question_text: A rigid container of helium gas is heated from 20°C to 40°C. The volume stays the same. A student measures the pressure and finds it has increased from 100 kPa to 107.3 kPa. Evaluate whether this result is reasonable using the particle model. (Assume 20°C = 293 K and 40°C = 313 K)
correct_answer: The result is reasonable. Expected pressure at 313 K should be: P₂ = 100 × (313/293) = 106.8 kPa. The measured 107.3 kPa is very close to this theoretical value. The small difference (0.5 kPa) is likely due to measurement error.
explanation: When temperature increases at constant volume, pressure increases proportionally (in Kelvin). We calculate what pressure should be using the ratio of temperatures: 313 K ÷ 293 K = 1.068, so pressure increases by 6.8%, giving 106.8 kPa. The student's measurement of 107.3 kPa is almost exactly right, showing that heating makes particles move faster and collide more frequently with the container walls.
